description: University track — Linear Algebra, Multivariable Calculus, and Ordinary Differential Equations (undergraduate level).

lessons.subject: Linear Algebra
lessons.subtopic: Magnitude, direction, unit vectors, and vector arithmetic
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can perform vector addition, scalar multiplication, find magnitude and unit vectors in 2D and 3D
lessons.prerequisite: Coordinate geometry, Pythagorean theorem
lessons.formula_spoken: The magnitude of v is the square root of v1 squared plus v2 squared plus v3 squared; the unit vector is v divided by its magnitude
lessons.narration_style: geometric and coordinate, building 3D intuition from 2D
lessons.seo_title: Vectors in 2D and 3D — Magnitude Direction Unit Vectors
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Arrows that carry SIZE and DIRECTION
lessons.real_world_hook: GPS satellite navigation, aircraft autopilot, and physics simulations all represent motion as vectors. Direction and magnitude together define where something is going and how fast.
lessons.concept_intuition: A vector is a directed arrow. Its length is the magnitude; its direction tells you where it points. Adding vectors means following one arrow then the next.
lessons.visual_hints: Draw 2D vectors as arrows. Show the parallelogram law for addition. Extend to 3D with axes. Show magnitude as Pythagoras in 3D. Show unit vector as arrow of length 1.
lessons.common_mistake: Confusing a vector ⟨3,4⟩ with the point (3,4). A vector represents a displacement — it has no fixed starting position.

lessons.subject: Linear Algebra
lessons.subtopic: Definition, geometric interpretation, and angle between vectors
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can compute the dot product, find the angle between two vectors, and test for orthogonality
lessons.prerequisite: Vectors, cosine rule, magnitudes
lessons.formula_spoken: The dot product equals a magnitude times b magnitude times cosine theta, which also equals the sum of products of corresponding components
lessons.narration_style: two formulas connected, geometric and algebraic
lessons.seo_title: Dot Product — Angle Between Vectors and Orthogonality
lessons.thumbnail_angle: One number that tells you the ANGLE
lessons.real_world_hook: Recommendation engines compute similarity between user preference vectors using the dot product. The higher the dot product, the more similar the tastes.
lessons.concept_intuition: The dot product measures how much two vectors point in the same direction. When they are perpendicular the dot product is zero — no shared direction at all.
lessons.visual_hints: Show two vectors with the angle θ between them. Show the projection of a onto b. Connect to the formula. Show three cases: θ=0 (parallel), θ=90° (orthogonal), θ=180° (antiparallel).
lessons.common_mistake: Thinking the dot product is a vector. It is a SCALAR — a single number, not a directed quantity.

lessons.subject: Linear Algebra
lessons.subtopic: Definition, perpendicular vector, and geometric area interpretation
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can compute the cross product, interpret it geometrically, and use it to find area of a parallelogram
lessons.prerequisite: Dot product, determinants, 3D vectors
lessons.formula_spoken: The cross product of a and b is computed using the 3 by 3 determinant with i j k in the first row, components of a in the second row, and components of b in the third row
lessons.narration_style: determinant method, right-hand rule, area application
lessons.seo_title: Cross Product — Perpendicular Vector and Parallelogram Area
lessons.thumbnail_angle: The vector perpendicular to BOTH arrows
lessons.real_world_hook: Computer graphics use the cross product to compute surface normals — the direction a polygon faces, needed for lighting and shading calculations in every 3D game.
lessons.concept_intuition: The cross product creates a new vector perpendicular to both a and b. Its magnitude equals the area of the parallelogram formed by a and b.
lessons.visual_hints: Show the 3×3 determinant expansion. Show the resulting vector perpendicular to both inputs using the right-hand rule. Show the parallelogram and label its area = |a×b|.
lessons.common_mistake: Cross product is not commutative: a×b = −(b×a). Reversing the order flips the direction of the resulting vector.

lessons.subject: Linear Algebra
lessons.subtopic: Row reduction to echelon form to solve linear systems
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can row-reduce an augmented matrix to echelon form and use back substitution to solve the system
lessons.prerequisite: Matrix notation, systems of linear equations
lessons.key_formula: \text{Row operations: swap, scale, replace} \Rightarrow \text{row echelon form}
lessons.formula_spoken: Use three row operations — swap two rows, multiply a row by a non-zero scalar, add a multiple of one row to another — to reach row echelon form
lessons.narration_style: algorithmic, showing each row operation clearly labeled
lessons.seo_title: Gaussian Elimination — Row Reduction Step by Step
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Solve ANY linear system with one algorithm
lessons.real_world_hook: Google Maps finds the optimal route by solving a massive linear system. The algorithm at its core is Gaussian elimination — running on millions of variables simultaneously.
lessons.concept_intuition: Gaussian elimination systematically eliminates variables from top to bottom. Each row operation does not change the solution set — it just makes the system simpler to read.
lessons.visual_hints: Show the augmented matrix [A|b]. Perform each row operation step-by-step, labelling each (R2 → R2 − 2R1, etc.). Show the final upper-triangular form and back-substitute.
lessons.common_mistake: Changing the right-hand-side column incorrectly. Every row operation must be applied to the ENTIRE augmented matrix including the b column.

lessons.subject: Linear Algebra
lessons.subtopic: Definition, axioms, subspaces
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can verify that a set with operations satisfies the vector space axioms and identify subspaces
lessons.prerequisite: Vectors, matrix operations, sets
lessons.key_formula: V \text{ is a vector space if } \mathbf{u}+\mathbf{v}\in V \text{ and } c\mathbf{u}\in V \text{ for all } \mathbf{u},\mathbf{v}\in V, c\in\mathbb{R}
lessons.formula_spoken: A vector space is closed under addition and scalar multiplication — combining any two elements or scaling any element stays inside the space
lessons.narration_style: abstract then concrete with examples and counterexamples
lessons.seo_title: Vector Spaces — Definition Axioms and Subspaces Explained
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Not just arrows — ANY set can be a vector space
lessons.real_world_hook: The set of all continuous functions forms a vector space. Adding two continuous functions gives another continuous function. Calculus operations work within this infinite-dimensional space.
lessons.concept_intuition: A vector space is any set where you can add elements and scale them, and the result stays in the set. ℝ², ℝ³, polynomials, and matrices are all vector spaces.
lessons.visual_hints: Show ℝ² as the familiar xy-plane vector space. Then show that a line through the origin is a subspace (closed under addition and scalar multiplication) but a line NOT through the origin is not.
lessons.common_mistake: Checking only closure without verifying the zero vector is in the subspace. A subspace must contain the zero vector — always check this first.

lessons.subject: Linear Algebra
lessons.subtopic: Spanning sets, linear independence, and basis definition
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can test vectors for linear independence, determine if they span a space, and identify a basis
lessons.prerequisite: Vector spaces, Gaussian elimination, systems of equations
lessons.formula_spoken: Vectors are linearly independent if the only solution to the linear combination equaling zero is all coefficients being zero
lessons.narration_style: geometric then algebraic, no redundant directions
lessons.seo_title: Linear Independence and Basis — Complete Explanation
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Minimum vectors to describe ALL of space
lessons.real_world_hook: In data science, finding a basis of independent features (no redundant information) is called dimensionality reduction — PCA is based directly on this concept.
lessons.concept_intuition: Linearly independent vectors each contribute a new direction. A basis is the smallest set of vectors that can reach every point in the space — no redundancy, no gaps.
lessons.visual_hints: In ℝ², show that ⟨1,0⟩ and ⟨0,1⟩ are independent (can reach all of ℝ²). Show that ⟨1,0⟩, ⟨0,1⟩, ⟨1,1⟩ are dependent (third is redundant).
lessons.common_mistake: Confusing 'a basis for ℝ²' with 'just two vectors'. They must also span the space — two parallel vectors do not form a basis even though there are two of them.

lessons.subject: Linear Algebra
lessons.subtopic: Definition, matrix representation, kernel and image
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can determine if a function is a linear transformation, find its matrix, and identify its kernel and image
lessons.prerequisite: Vector spaces, matrix multiplication, linear independence
lessons.formula_spoken: T is linear if T of u plus v equals T of u plus T of v, and T of c u equals c times T of u
lessons.narration_style: visual transformation of the plane, showing what matrices do to space
lessons.seo_title: Linear Transformations — Matrix Representation Kernel Image
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Matrices are FUNCTIONS that transform space
lessons.real_world_hook: Every image filter, rotation, zoom, and shear in Photoshop is a linear transformation applied to pixel coordinates. The entire photo editing pipeline is matrix multiplication.
lessons.concept_intuition: A linear transformation is a function that preserves the vector space structure. It maps lines to lines and keeps the origin fixed. Every linear transformation has a matrix representation.
lessons.visual_hints: Show the standard basis vectors e₁ and e₂ being transformed. The images T(e₁) and T(e₂) form the COLUMNS of the transformation matrix. Animate a square being sheared or rotated.
lessons.common_mistake: Thinking all functions between vector spaces are linear. T(x)=x+1 is NOT linear because T(0)=1≠0.

lessons.subject: Linear Algebra
lessons.subtopic: Characteristic polynomial and eigenspace
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can find eigenvalues from the characteristic polynomial and compute eigenvectors for each
lessons.prerequisite: Determinants, systems of equations, matrix multiplication
lessons.formula_spoken: v is an eigenvector of A with eigenvalue lambda if A times v equals lambda times v; eigenvalues satisfy the characteristic equation det of A minus lambda I equals zero
lessons.narration_style: revealing the special directions that matrices preserve
lessons.seo_title: Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors — Characteristic Polynomial Method
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Directions a matrix CANNOT rotate
lessons.real_world_hook: Google's PageRank algorithm ranks every web page by computing the dominant eigenvector of a massive matrix. The page with the largest eigenvector component ranks first.
lessons.concept_intuition: An eigenvector is a special direction that the matrix only scales — does not rotate. The scaling factor is the eigenvalue. Stretching by λ in the direction v.
lessons.visual_hints: Show a matrix stretching the plane. Draw arrows showing most vectors being rotated. Then highlight one special vector that only scales along its direction — that is the eigenvector.
lessons.common_mistake: Using I instead of λI in the characteristic equation. It must be det(A − λI) = 0, not det(A) − λ = 0.

lessons.subject: Linear Algebra
lessons.subtopic: Diagonalizable matrices and the decomposition A = PDP⁻¹
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can determine if a matrix is diagonalizable and compute the decomposition A=PDP⁻¹
lessons.prerequisite: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors, matrix inverse, matrix multiplication
lessons.formula_spoken: A equals P D P inverse, where D is the diagonal matrix of eigenvalues and P is the matrix of corresponding eigenvectors as columns
lessons.narration_style: connecting eigenvalues to diagonal form
lessons.seo_title: Diagonalization — A = PDP Inverse Step by Step
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Simplify a matrix to its diagonal skeleton
lessons.real_world_hook: Computing Aⁿ (matrix powers) is expensive. Diagonalization turns it into Dⁿ — trivially raise each diagonal entry to the power n. Population genetics models use Aⁿ repeatedly.
lessons.concept_intuition: If a matrix has n independent eigenvectors, change basis to those eigenvectors. In the new basis the matrix is diagonal — it just scales each direction by its eigenvalue.
lessons.visual_hints: Show the three matrices P, D, P⁻¹. Show that columns of P are eigenvectors. Show multiplication AA=P D² P⁻¹ to demonstrate computing powers is easy with diagonal form.
lessons.common_mistake: Forgetting to check that eigenvectors are linearly independent before claiming diagonalizability. Repeated eigenvalues may not yield enough independent eigenvectors.

lessons.subject: Linear Algebra
lessons.subtopic: Gram-Schmidt orthogonalisation process
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can apply the Gram-Schmidt process to convert any basis into an orthonormal basis
lessons.prerequisite: Dot product, projections, linear independence
lessons.formula_spoken: To orthogonalise, subtract from each vector all its projections onto the previously computed orthogonal vectors
lessons.narration_style: geometric removal of components, iterative
lessons.seo_title: Gram-Schmidt Process — Building Orthonormal Basis Step by Step
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Turn any basis into perpendicular arrows
lessons.real_world_hook: Noise-cancelling headphones use orthogonal signal decomposition to separate speech from background noise. Gram-Schmidt is the mathematical engine behind the separation.
lessons.concept_intuition: Start with any basis vector. For each new vector, subtract the parts pointing in directions you have already handled. What remains is perpendicular to all previous vectors.
lessons.visual_hints: Show two non-orthogonal vectors. Project v₂ onto v₁. Subtract the projection from v₂ to get an orthogonal vector. Normalize both to unit length. Show the resulting orthonormal pair.
lessons.common_mistake: Normalizing before completing all the orthogonalization steps. Normalise each vector only after computing all of its projections.

practice: \text{Normalize each to get the orthonormal basis: }

lessons.subject: Multivariable Calculus
lessons.subtopic: Definition, computation, and geometric interpretation
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can compute first and second partial derivatives and interpret them as rates of change in one direction
lessons.prerequisite: Single-variable calculus, functions of two variables
lessons.formula_spoken: The partial derivative with respect to x is the limit of f at x plus h comma y minus f at x comma y, all over h — treating y as a constant
lessons.narration_style: slicing the 3D surface with planes
lessons.seo_title: Partial Derivatives — Functions of Two Variables Explained
lessons.thumbnail_angle: How does a surface slope in ONE direction?
lessons.real_world_hook: A weather model computes how temperature changes eastward (∂T/∂x) and northward (∂T/∂y) independently at every grid point. Partial derivatives power every climate simulation.
lessons.concept_intuition: A partial derivative holds all other variables constant and differentiates with respect to just one. Slice the 3D surface with a vertical plane parallel to one axis — the slope of that slice is the partial derivative.
lessons.visual_hints: Show a 3D surface z=f(x,y). Slice it with planes x=constant and y=constant. Show the 2D curve on each slice. The slope of those curves is the partial derivative.
lessons.common_mistake: Differentiating every variable when computing a partial. Treat ALL variables except the one you are differentiating with respect to as CONSTANTS.

lessons.subject: Multivariable Calculus
lessons.subtopic: The gradient vector and steepest ascent direction
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can compute the gradient, find the directional derivative, and identify the direction of steepest ascent
lessons.prerequisite: Partial derivatives, unit vectors, dot product
lessons.key_formula: \nabla f=\left\langle\frac{\partial f}{\partial x},\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\right\rangle,\quad D_{\hat{u}}f=\nabla f\cdot\hat{u}
lessons.formula_spoken: The gradient is the vector of partial derivatives; the directional derivative in direction u-hat is the dot product of the gradient with u-hat
lessons.narration_style: gradient as a compass pointing uphill on a topographic map
lessons.seo_title: Gradient and Directional Derivatives — Steepest Ascent Explained
lessons.thumbnail_angle: The arrow pointing UPHILL on any surface
lessons.real_world_hook: Machine learning's gradient descent minimizes the loss function by stepping opposite the gradient — the steepest downhill direction. Every AI model is trained using this concept.
lessons.concept_intuition: The gradient is a vector pointing in the direction of steepest increase. Its magnitude is the rate of that increase. Move opposite the gradient to descend fastest.
lessons.visual_hints: Show a topographic map (contours of equal height). Draw gradient vectors perpendicular to contours pointing uphill. Show that the directional derivative is maximized along the gradient.
lessons.common_mistake: Thinking the gradient is a scalar. The gradient is a VECTOR — it has both magnitude and direction.

lessons.subject: Multivariable Calculus
lessons.subtopic: Critical points, second derivative test, saddle points
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can find critical points of f(x,y) and classify them using the second derivative test (Hessian determinant)
lessons.prerequisite: Gradient, partial derivatives, single-variable critical points
lessons.formula_spoken: D equals f sub xx times f sub yy minus f sub xy squared; D positive with f sub xx positive means a local minimum
lessons.narration_style: systematic: find critical points, compute Hessian, classify
lessons.seo_title: Multivariable Optimization — Critical Points and Hessian Test
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Min Max or Saddle? The Hessian decides
lessons.real_world_hook: An electronics company minimizes the cost of a rectangular box (volume fixed, minimize surface area) using multivariable optimization — two variables, one constraint.
lessons.concept_intuition: Set both partial derivatives to zero to find critical points. The Hessian D acts as a 2D version of the second derivative test: D>0 means it curves the same way in all directions (extremum), D<0 means it curves differently (saddle).
lessons.visual_hints: Show a 3D surface with a minimum (bowl), maximum (cap), and saddle point. Compute D for each and show the sign confirms the type.
lessons.common_mistake: Not checking both conditions when D>0 — you also need the sign of f_xx to determine if it is a max (f_xx<0) or min (f_xx>0).

lessons.subject: Multivariable Calculus
lessons.subtopic: Iterated integrals over rectangular and general regions
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can evaluate double integrals over rectangular and general regions using iterated integration
lessons.prerequisite: Single-variable integration, area between curves, FTC
lessons.formula_spoken: The double integral over R equals the iterated integral — integrate with respect to y first from c to d, then integrate with respect to x from a to b
lessons.narration_style: inside-out integration, building volume from area
lessons.seo_title: Double Integrals — Iterated Integration Over 2D Regions
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Integrate over a 2D region — volume!
lessons.real_world_hook: Computing the mass of a thin plate with variable density requires a double integral. Every MRI scan uses double integration to reconstruct the density of tissue from signals.
lessons.concept_intuition: A double integral sums infinitely many thin columns of height f(x,y). Compute the inner integral to sum along columns, then the outer integral to sum all those column sums.
lessons.visual_hints: Show a 3D solid under a surface. Slice into strips parallel to y-axis. Show the inner integral computing each strip's area. Outer integral stacks all strips.
lessons.common_mistake: Integrating in the wrong order without adjusting the limits. When switching integration order, always re-determine the new limits by sketching the region.

lessons.subject: Ordinary Differential Equations
lessons.subtopic: Integrating factor method
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can solve any first-order linear ODE using the integrating factor technique
lessons.prerequisite: Separable ODEs, integration, exponential functions
lessons.formula_spoken: For y prime plus P of x times y equals Q of x, the integrating factor mu equals e to the integral of P dx
lessons.narration_style: four steps: compute mu, multiply, recognise product rule, integrate
lessons.seo_title: Linear First-Order ODE — Integrating Factor Method
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Solve ANY linear ODE in 4 steps
lessons.real_world_hook: RC electrical circuits obey V'+V/RC=E(t)/RC — a linear first-order ODE. The integrating factor gives the exact voltage at any time, essential for circuit design.
lessons.concept_intuition: Multiply both sides by μ=e^∫P(x)dx. The left side collapses to d/dx[μy] by the product rule. Then integrate both sides to get μy = ∫μQ dx, and solve for y.
lessons.visual_hints: Four-step checklist on the board. Label each step clearly. Show how multiplying by μ makes the left side a perfect derivative using the product rule.
lessons.common_mistake: Not simplifying to standard form y'+P(x)y=Q(x) first. If the equation is 2y'+4xy=8 you must divide by 2 before computing the integrating factor.

lessons.subject: Ordinary Differential Equations
lessons.subtopic: Characteristic equation and general solution
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can solve second-order linear homogeneous ODEs with constant coefficients using the characteristic equation
lessons.prerequisite: First-order ODEs, quadratic equations, exponential functions
lessons.formula_spoken: For a y double prime plus b y prime plus c y equals zero, substitute y equals e to the r x and solve the characteristic equation a r squared plus b r plus c equals zero
lessons.narration_style: three cases from discriminant — real distinct, repeated, complex
lessons.seo_title: Second-Order Homogeneous ODEs — Characteristic Equation
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Solve spring-mass equations with one formula
lessons.real_world_hook: A mass on a spring obeys mx''+bx'+kx=0. The characteristic equation gives the natural frequencies of oscillation — used in every mechanical and civil engineering design.
lessons.concept_intuition: Guess y=eˣ. Substituting into the ODE converts a differential equation into a simple algebra equation (the characteristic equation). Solve for r to find all solutions.
lessons.visual_hints: Show the three cases: (1) Two real distinct roots → y=C₁eʳ¹ˣ+C₂eʳ²ˣ. (2) Repeated root → y=(C₁+C₂x)eʳˣ. (3) Complex roots α±βi → y=eᵅˣ(C₁cosβx+C₂sinβx). One example of each.
lessons.common_mistake: Using the particular solution form y=eˣ to try to solve non-homogeneous equations. For the homogeneous case this works; the non-homogeneous case needs an extra particular solution.

lessons.subject: Ordinary Differential Equations
lessons.subtopic: Finding particular solutions for non-homogeneous ODEs
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can choose the correct form of the particular solution for polynomials, exponentials, and sinusoidal forcing functions
lessons.prerequisite: Second-order homogeneous ODEs, differentiation
lessons.key_formula: y = y_h + y_p,\quad y_h\text{ solves homogeneous},\;y_p\text{ solves particular}
lessons.formula_spoken: The general solution is y sub h plus y sub p — the homogeneous solution plus any particular solution to the full non-homogeneous equation
lessons.narration_style: guess table approach, three types of forcing function
lessons.seo_title: Undetermined Coefficients — Non-Homogeneous ODE Particular Solution
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Guess the solution — then PROVE it works
lessons.real_world_hook: A tuning fork forces a guitar string at its frequency. The particular solution models the driven oscillation — resonance occurs when the forcing frequency matches the natural frequency.
lessons.concept_intuition: Match the form of y_p to the form of the right-hand side. Polynomial driving → polynomial y_p. Exponential driving → exponential y_p. Sinusoidal driving → sinusoidal y_p.
lessons.visual_hints: Guess table: if Q(x)=polynomial degree n, try Aₙxⁿ+...+A₀. If Q=Aeᵅˣ, try Beᵅˣ. If Q=Asinβx, try Csinβx+Dcosβx. Show modification rule for resonance cases.
lessons.common_mistake: Guessing y_p = e^{2x} when e^{2x} is already part of y_h. In that case multiply by x — the modification rule applies.

lessons.subject: Ordinary Differential Equations
lessons.subtopic: Definition, standard table, and properties
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can compute Laplace transforms of basic functions using the definition and standard table
lessons.prerequisite: Improper integrals, exponential functions, ODEs
lessons.formula_spoken: The Laplace transform of f of t is F of s, defined as the integral from zero to infinity of e to the negative s t times f of t dt
lessons.narration_style: transforming from time domain to s-domain
lessons.seo_title: Laplace Transform — Definition Table and Properties
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Turn differential equations into ALGEBRA
lessons.real_world_hook: Control engineers design autopilot systems using Laplace transforms. Differential equations describing the aircraft's dynamics become simple algebra in the s-domain — much easier to work with.
lessons.concept_intuition: The Laplace transform converts a function of time into a function of the complex variable s. Derivatives become multiplication by s. Differential equations become algebraic equations.
lessons.visual_hints: Show the integral definition. Compute L{1} and L{eᵅᵗ} directly. Then display the standard table of pairs. Show the time-derivative property: L{f'}=sF(s)−f(0).
lessons.common_mistake: Applying Laplace transform to problems without zero initial conditions without accounting for the initial value terms in L{f'}=sF(s)−f(0).

lessons.subject: Ordinary Differential Equations
lessons.subtopic: Transform, solve algebraically, invert
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can solve an IVP by applying the Laplace transform, solving the algebraic equation, and inverting
lessons.prerequisite: Laplace transform table, partial fractions, second-order ODEs
lessons.formula_spoken: Apply the Laplace transform to convert the ODE to algebra, solve for F of s, then invert using the table to get y of t
lessons.narration_style: three-lane highway: transform, solve, invert
lessons.seo_title: Solving ODEs with Laplace Transform — IVP Step by Step
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Convert ODE to algebra — then convert back
lessons.real_world_hook: Electrical engineers use Laplace to solve the RLC circuit ODE. The transform turns it into F(s)=voltage/impedance — simple algebra — then invert for current vs time.
lessons.concept_intuition: The power of Laplace is that initial conditions are automatically included. Transform the equation, solve for F(s) using algebra, then find the inverse transform from the table.
lessons.visual_hints: Show the three steps as a flowchart. Work a complete IVP on the board. Use partial fractions to decompose F(s) into table-invertible pieces.
lessons.common_mistake: Inverting without decomposing into recognisable table forms. Always use partial fractions to break F(s) into pieces that match the standard table entries.

lessons.subject: Ordinary Differential Equations
lessons.subtopic: Solving 2×2 systems using eigenvalues
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can write a system of ODEs in matrix form and solve using eigenvalues and eigenvectors
lessons.prerequisite: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors, second-order ODEs, Laplace transform
lessons.formula_spoken: The solution to x prime equals Ax is a linear combination of eigenvectors times e to the eigenvalue t
lessons.narration_style: connecting linear algebra eigenvalue theory to ODE solutions
lessons.seo_title: Systems of ODEs — Eigenvalue Method Step by Step
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Linear algebra meets differential equations
lessons.real_world_hook: Predator-prey population models (Lotka-Volterra) are systems of ODEs. Near equilibrium they are linear — solved by the eigenvalue method to predict oscillating population cycles.
lessons.concept_intuition: A system of two ODEs can be written as a single matrix equation x'=Ax. The solution is built from eigenvectors of A, each multiplied by eˡᵗ where λ is the corresponding eigenvalue.
lessons.visual_hints: Show x'=Ax. Guess x=veˡᵗ. Substituting gives Av=λv — the eigenvalue equation. Solve for eigenvalues and eigenvectors. Combine to build the general solution.
lessons.common_mistake: Forgetting to multiply by both the eigenvector AND the exponential. The solution is cᵢvᵢeˡⁱᵗ — the eigenvector is not alone.
